sections.title: Intake
sections.conclusion: Baseline fields and source anchors establish whether the proposal can be reviewed at all.

sections.title: Claims and evidence
findings: Proposal document read by reasoning layer: yes
Requested: Cardano Builder DAO treasury withdrawal to continue and expand a smart contract-governed, KPI-driven funding mechanism for Cardano builders aligned with Vision 2030 metrics.
Recipient: Cardano Builder DAO, with funds initially received and administered by an independent council/multisig composed of dRep DAO members before milestone-based disbursement.

Deliverables: Allocate capital to builders expected to improve Cardano KPIs including monthly active users, monthly on-chain transactions, and TVL., Require funded projects to track core KPIs: monthly active users, TVL, and on-chain transactions., Continue development of KPI tracking toward live on-chain Cardano data and repeated KPI reporting cadence., Operate review, debate, temperature check, final voting, milestone validation, and transparent disbursement processes for funded projects., Return unused or unallocated DAO treasury funds to the Cardano Treasury after completion of approved work, governance processes, or funding activities.

Claim (governance, supported_in_proposal, high materiality): The Cardano Builder DAO is proposed as an ecosystem-wide, smart contract-governed funding mechanism to support projects advancing Cardano Vision 2030 through measurable KPIs.

Claim (adoption, supported_in_proposal, high materiality): The proposal identifies monthly active users, monthly on-chain transactions, and TVL as the core KPIs its funded projects are intended to improve and track.
Claim (technical, proposer_asserted, medium materiality): The DAO says it built Version 1 and Version 2 of a KPI dashboard, moving from self-reported KPI tracking toward live on-chain Cardano data.
Claim (economic, independently_verifiable, high materiality): The Cardano Builder DAO claims it has already distributed 11.1 million ADA across 34 proposals in two funding rounds.

Claim (governance, independently_verifiable, high materiality): Round 1 is claimed to have included 38 voting members, 27 requesting members, 20 funded companies, 83% governance participation, and 5,541,335 ADA distributed through a smart contract-governed process.

Claim (governance, independently_verifiable, high materiality): Round 2 is claimed to have added 18 new members, funded 14 companies, and achieved 88% member participation after review, debate, temperature checks, and final voting.
missing: Independent evidence for: The DAO says it built Version 1 and Version 2 of a KPI dashboard, moving from self-reported KPI tracking toward live on-chain Cardano data.

Independent evidence for: The DAO claims it created and amended governing documents, elected a board, added third-party assurance, implemented merit-based proposal review, and executed treasury withdrawals on-chain with transparency.
sections.conclusion: Each claim must map to replayable public evidence; proposer assertions alone are not enough for confidence.

sections.title: Treasury analysis
findings: Six-month treasury flow regime: stressed (basis: total inflow (tau + donations) vs enacted withdrawals)
sections.conclusion: Treasury votes require a higher bar because they consume shared ADA and create precedent.

sections.title: Risk review
sections.conclusion: Risk is not a side note; unmitigated execution or governance risk can dominate an otherwise attractive proposal.

sections.title: Counterargument pass
findings: Strongest YES: the proposal substantiates "The Cardano Builder DAO is proposed as an ecosystem-wide, smart contract-governed funding mechanism to support projects advancing Cardano Vision 2030 through measurable KPIs." and clears the evidence gates.

Strongest NO: a material claim is unsupported — "The DAO says it built Version 1 and Version 2 of a KPI dashboard, moving from self-reported KPI tracking toward live on-chain Cardano data." — so cost or precedent may outweigh the benefit.
Strongest hold: if claims cannot be tied to replayable evidence, abstaining avoids overclaiming certainty.
sections.conclusion: A defensible rationale must show the best opposing case before it reaches a vote.

sections.title: Synthesis
findings: Final vote must be derived from completed sections above, not from a prose summary.
The public rationale should name the decisive section and the strongest counterargument.
sections.conclusion: The vote is only credible if the assessment tree shows enough work for a skeptical delegator to audit.
